title: Average Pooling: Summarizing the Region
concept: Average pooling replaces each local window with its mean, giving a smoother summary of the region than max pooling.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does max pooling keep from each local window?
exercises.explain: Right. Max pooling keeps the winner. Average pooling uses a different fixed summary rule.

cards.title: Top score vs. class average
cards.body: If one student scores 100 but the rest score 60, the top score and the class average tell you different stories. Pooling works the same way. Max pooling tells you the strongest spike. Average pooling tells you the overall level in the region.

cards.title: What is average pooling?
cards.body: Average pooling looks at a local window and outputs the mean of its values. Instead of asking, "What was the single strongest activation here?" it asks, "What was the overall average strength in this region?"

cards.title: Blending the whole bowl
cards.body: Imagine tasting a pot of soup. You do not keep only the strongest spice crystal you find. You stir the whole spoonful together and judge the overall flavor. Average pooling does that with activations.

cards.title: Same window, different summary
cards.body: Use the same window as yesterday: [1, 5; 2, 4]. Max pooling gives 5, because it keeps the biggest value. Average pooling gives 3, because (1 + 5 + 2 + 4) / 4 = 3. Same input window, different question.

cards.title: Average is not "most common"
cards.body: Average pooling uses the arithmetic mean, not the most frequent value. In a window like [1, 5; 2, 4], the answer is 3 even though 3 never appeared in the original window.

cards.title: Max vs. Average on the Same Window
cards.body: Two fixed rules, two different summaries:

exercises.prompt: If an average-pooling window contains [2, 6; 4, 8], what value comes out?
True or false: Average pooling will always output a larger number than max pooling on the same window.
exercises.explain: False. Max pooling is always at least as large as the average on the same set of numbers.

exercises.sentence: Average pooling uses the ___ of the values in the window.
exercises.explain: Mean is the math word for average.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best describes average pooling?

options.text: It summarizes the whole local region by averaging the values together
It copies only the largest value and ignores the rest
It creates new learned filters for each region

exercises.explain: Exactly — average pooling blends the region into one mean value.

exercises.prompt: A designer is comparing two pooling rules on the same window [1, 5; 2, 4]. They want the output to reflect the overall activity of the region, not just the single strongest spike. Which choice fits best, and why?

options.text: Average pooling, because it uses all four values to produce one mean summary of the region
Max pooling, because it turns the whole region into the smallest value
Convolution, because pooling cannot summarize values at all
Average pooling, because it always outputs the most common number in the window

exercises.explain: Exactly. Average pooling is the better match when you care about the overall level across the whole region. Next: sometimes we do not summarize a small patch — we summarize the entire feature map at once.
